content.text: Why Autoscaling Is Hard for LLMs
LLM inference differs from typical microservice scaling in two ways that make autoscaling harder:
(a 4096-token generation takes 50× longer than a 64-token generation at batch size 1). Standard CPU/memory-based HPA cannot capture either.
Effective LLM autoscaling requires: (1) the right metric, (2) tuned stabilisation windows, and (3) proactive warm-up to absorb cold-start latency.
The metric determines what triggers scaling. For LLM serving, the most predictive metrics are:
Best overall: accounts for variable request length
Works for async/batch APIs; not streaming

metric — the number of requests currently in-flight per serving pod — is the most direct measure of serving pressure. It naturally accounts for request heterogeneity because long requests occupy a slot for longer.

# KEDA ScaledObject: scale vLLM Deployment on avg in-flight requests
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: vllm-autoscaler
  namespace: llm-serving
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: vllm-llama-70b
  minReplicaCount: 1          # always-on minimum (avoids cold-start on first request)
  maxReplicaCount: 12
  cooldownPeriod: 300         # seconds before scale-down fires (prevents flapping)
  pollingInterval: 15         # check metrics every 15s
  advanced:
    horizontalPodAutoscalerConfig:
      behavior:
        scaleUp:
          stabilizationWindowSeconds: 30    # scale up fast
          policies:
            - type: Pods
              value: 2
              periodSeconds: 60             # add at most 2 pods per minute
        scaleDown:
          stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # scale down slowly (5 min window)
          policies:
            - type: Pods
              value: 1
              periodSeconds: 120            # remove at most 1 pod per 2 min
  triggers:
    - type: prometheus
      metadata:
        serverAddress: http://prometheus.monitoring.svc:9090
        metricName: vllm_avg_ongoing_requests_per_replica
        threshold: "20"       # scale up when avg > 20 in-flight requests per pod
        query: |
          avg(
            vllm:num_requests_running{
              namespace="llm-serving",
              deployment="vllm-llama-70b"
            }
          )

# KEDA ScaledObject: scale on Redis queue depth (async completion API)
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: vllm-async-autoscaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: vllm-async-worker
  minReplicaCount: 0          # scale to zero when queue is empty
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  triggers:
    - type: redis
      metadata:
        address: redis.cache.svc:6379
        listName: llm_generation_queue
        listLength: "5"       # 1 replica per 5 queued requests
        activationListLength: "1"  # wake from zero when ≥1 item in queue
) eliminates GPU cost during idle periods but introduces a cold-start gap when the first request arrives. Use zero-scaling only for background batch jobs, not interactive APIs with latency SLOs.
Ray Serve has a built-in autoscaler that tracks
and adjusts replica count declaratively. It is tightly integrated with Ray's actor model, so scaling decisions happen within the same Python process that manages serving:

from ray import serve

@serve.deployment(
    autoscaling_config={
        "min_replicas": 1,
        "max_replicas": 10,
        "initial_replicas": 2,
        # Scale up when ongoing requests per replica exceeds this
        "target_num_ongoing_requests_per_replica": 20,
        # Wait this long before scaling up (avoids reacting to spikes)
        "upscale_delay_s": 30.0,
        # Wait this long before scaling down (avoids premature scale-down)
        "downscale_delay_s": 300.0,
        # Look-back window for averaging the metric
        "metrics_interval_s": 10.0,
        "look_back_period_s": 30.0,
    },
    ray_actor_options={"num_gpus": 2},   # each replica gets 2 GPUs
    max_ongoing_requests=40,              # queue depth cap per replica
)
class VLLMDeployment:
    def __init__(self):
        from vllm import AsyncLLMEngine, AsyncEngineArgs
        self.engine = AsyncLLMEngine.from_engine_args(
            AsyncEngineArgs(model="meta-llama/Llama-2-70b-chat-hf",
                            tensor_parallel_size=2)
        )

    async def __call__(self, request):
        # ... generate response
        pass

app = VLLMDeployment.bind()
serve.run(app)

Pod autoscalers add replicas; node autoscalers provision the GPU nodes those replicas land on. Karpenter (AWS) responds to pending pod events and selects the cheapest instance type that satisfies the pod's GPU request:

# Karpenter NodePool for GPU inference burst
apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1beta1
kind: NodePool
metadata:
  name: gpu-burst
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        workload: llm-inference
      taints:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          value: "present"
          effect: NoSchedule
    spec:
      nodeClassRef:
        name: gpu-nodeclass
      requirements:
        - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
          operator: In
          values:
            - g5.xlarge       # A10G 24GB  — for 7B INT4
            - g5.2xlarge      # A10G 24GB  — more CPU/RAM
            - p3.2xlarge      # V100 16GB  — fallback
        - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type
          operator: In
          values: ["spot", "on-demand"]   # prefer spot, fall back to on-demand
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 32      # max GPUs this NodePool will provision
  disruption:
    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty
    consolidateAfter: 10m   # reclaim idle GPU nodes after 10 min
feature terminates underutilised GPU nodes after
. Setting this too aggressively (< 5 min) causes a cycle of terminate-provision-terminate when traffic is bursty. GPU nodes are expensive; 10–30 minutes is a safer floor.
Reactive autoscaling always trails demand — by the time a metric exceeds its threshold, some requests are already degraded. Two patterns reduce this lag:

# Scale up before known traffic peaks (e.g., business hours)
# Using KEDA CronTrigger
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: vllm-scheduled-autoscaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: vllm-llama-7b
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  triggers:
    # Business hours: scale floor to 4 replicas proactively
    - type: cron
      metadata:
        timezone: America/New_York
        start: "0 8 * * 1-5"    # 8 AM Mon-Fri
        end:   "0 20 * * 1-5"   # 8 PM Mon-Fri
        desiredReplicas: "4"
    # Off-hours: scale back to 1
    - type: cron
      metadata:
        timezone: America/New_York
        start: "0 20 * * 1-5"
        end:   "0 8 * * 1-5"
        desiredReplicas: "1"
    # Always active: queue-depth reactive scaling
    - type: redis
      metadata:
        address: redis:6379
        listName: llm_queue
        listLength: "10"
Model weight loading (60–120 seconds for 70B) means a newly scheduled pod isn't useful until it's fully initialised. Use
to keep at least one warm replica, and
to ensure the load balancer only routes traffic once the model is loaded:

# vLLM Pod readiness probe — only marks ready after model weights are loaded
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 30
  periodSeconds: 20
  failureThreshold: 6
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 90    # 70B model needs ~90s to load
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 6
  successThreshold: 1
# Pod is excluded from Service endpoints until readiness passes
# Kubernetes does NOT route traffic to Pending/Initializing pods
The metrics pipeline connects vLLM Prometheus metrics to KEDA. The standard stack:
vLLM's OpenAI-compatible server exports Prometheus metrics at
on port 8000 by default. Key metric:
endpoint of every vLLM pod every 15 seconds.
A recording rule computes per-deployment averages to reduce query latency.
KEDA's Prometheus trigger queries the recording rule every

# Prometheus recording rule (reduces query latency for KEDA)
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: vllm-recording-rules
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
    - name: vllm
      interval: 15s
      rules:
        - record: vllm:num_requests_running:avg_by_deployment
          expr: |
            avg by (deployment, namespace) (
              vllm:num_requests_running
            )

        - record: vllm:num_requests_waiting:avg_by_deployment
          expr: |
            avg by (deployment, namespace) (
              vllm:num_requests_waiting
            )

        - alert: VLLMHighQueueDepth
          expr: vllm:num_requests_waiting:avg_by_deployment > 50
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: "vLLM queue backed up — autoscaler may be too slow"
: scale up, then immediately down, then up again. GPU pods take 60–120 seconds to start, so flapping wastes money and degrades the user experience.
The desired replica count formula includes a stabilisation window:
desired count over the window duration, preventing a transient metric dip from triggering an immediate scale-down. Tuning guidelines:
GPU startup latency; avoid premature removal
Terminating a node incurs new startup cost
Zero-scale saves cost; adds cold-start for first request
Hard ceiling on GPU spend; set per cluster GPU quota

GPU util is a lagging indicator. A burst of 100 simultaneous 4096-token requests fills the request queue immediately, but GPU util climbs slowly as decode begins. Queue depth or ongoing_requests react faster.
Forgetting model download time in readinessProbe:
is too low, Kubernetes marks the pod ready before the model is loaded and routes requests to it, causing errors. Measure actual startup time and set
of 30s means KEDA sees a 30-second lag. Set it to 10–15s for LLM serving.
Adding 2 pods per 60s but removing 1 per 120s is intentionally asymmetric (fast up, slow down). Symmetric rates cause flapping under variable load.
Without a PDB, Kubernetes can terminate all pods simultaneously during scale-down. Set
to ensure at least one replica stays up for in-flight requests.

LLM autoscaling requires metrics that reflect request load (avg_ongoing_requests, queue depth) rather than infrastructure metrics (CPU, GPU util). KEDA provides Kubernetes-native scaling on Prometheus and queue metrics with tunable stabilisation windows. Ray Serve's built-in autoscaler simplifies deployment when already using Ray. Node-level autoscaling via Karpenter must account for GPU startup time; consolidation windows of 10–30 minutes prevent costly terminate-provision cycles. Schedule-based proactive scaling handles predictable diurnal patterns; readinessProbe ensures new replicas only receive traffic after model weights are fully loaded.
